topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope, thick and cream-colored, sat accusingly on my desk. It was addressed in elegant, looping script, and the date inside confirmed it had been sealed for nearly seven years. My hands, usually steady, trembled slightly as I slid a thumbnail under the flap. Inside, the words began: "Please accept my sincerest apologies for the profound hurt I caused you..." I read the first sentence twice, a sharp, unpleasant clenching in my chest. I crumpled the paper, the sound a violent tearing in the quiet office. How dare they think a few carefully chosen sentences could erase the wreckage? I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my mahogany desk, my jaw tight enough to crack. Every muscle in my body felt coiled, ready to spring. I wanted to smash the letter against the wall, to feel something immediate and satisfyingly destructive.

[story 2]

It arrived on a Tuesday, unannounced, tucked into the mail slot. I hadn't expected anything, but the weight of the stationary in my hands felt like a physical burden. When I finally unfolded it, the ink seemed to swim before my eyes. A formal admission of fault. A wave of hot, churning anger rose from my stomach, making my breath catch in my throat. I reread the paragraphs, focusing intently on the carefully constructed phrasing. Each word felt like a deliberate slight, a flimsy attempt to smooth over something fundamentally broken. I dropped the letter onto the floor. My feet moved automatically, pacing the room in tight, angry circles. I clenched my fists until my knuckles were white, the tension vibrating through my arms. I couldn't look at the window; the world outside seemed too calm, too indifferent to the seismic disturbance happening inside me.

[story 3]

I found the letter tucked inside a book I hadn't touched in years. The paper felt brittle, aged, and the sheer audacity of it—receiving correspondence from that person after all this time—made my skin prickle with hostility. I didn't bother with pleasantries. I tore the letter open with a swift, jerky motion, letting the sound of the ripping paper be my only response. The contents were a parade of contrite phrases, hollow and insincere. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat. I stood by the window, staring out at the rain-slicked street, my knuckles white where I gripped the edge of the sill. My breathing was shallow and rapid. This wasn't relief; it was a fresh surge of pure, unadulterated fury, a burning sensation behind my eyes that demanded immediate, physical release.
